title: Greg Treat Calls Out FTS and Slanders Us on the Senate Floor
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds point-by-point to Oklahoma Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat, who attacked Free the States from the Senate floor for harsh rhetoric in Silberman's article opposing Treat's stance on HB1236. Silberman defends his tone biblically, denies any threats of violence, and turns the criticism back on Treat for keeping abortion legal.
audiences: abolitionist activists weighing how harshly to confront politicians
pro-life Christians uncertain whether sharp rebuke is unchristlike
Oklahoma voters evaluating Greg Treat's leadership
state legislators considering nullification bills like HB1236
journalists covering church-state rhetoric in the abortion debate

questions.question: What kind of 'threats' does Free the States actually make against legislators?
questions.audience: abolitionist activists weighing how harshly to confront politicians
questions.answer: Silberman acknowledges only one kind of threat: the political threat to replace officials who refuse to obey Christ or uphold the Constitution. He explicitly denies that FTS has ever threatened violence or serious harm and calls any such insinuation slander.
questions.quote: If by “threats,” he means threats to replace politicians who don’t obey Christ and uphold the Constitution, then sure. We make that kind of threat. If they won’t obey Christ or uphold the Constitution, then they should be replaced by people who will. But Treat seems to be insinuating that we deliver some sort of threat of violence or otherwise serious harm against the State and federal legislators. That would be slander. We have never done any such thing.

questions.question: Who has actually threatened violence in this dispute, according to Silberman?
questions.audience: Oklahoma voters evaluating Greg Treat's leadership
questions.answer: Silberman flips the accusation, pointing to pro-life Republican senators McCortney and Boggs as the ones who have made violent threats against abolitionists, while Treat and others play victim without offering specifics.
questions.quote: On the other hand, pro-life Republican Senator Greg McCortney and former pro-life Republican Senator Larry Boggs have both [threatened violence against abolitionists]

questions.question: Why does Silberman think vague accusations of threats should be dismissed?
questions.audience: journalists covering church-state rhetoric in the abortion debate
questions.answer: He argues that a recurring tactic of pro-life establishment legislators is to allege threats while refusing to provide details, using victimhood to deflect from their support for keeping abortion legal. Without specifics, he says, the claims and the people making them lose credibility.
questions.quote: Treat needs to give details of what he’s talking about or his accusations should be disregarded. Claiming threats of violence against them and refusing to give details has been a common tactic of pro-life establishment legislators like Treat, McCortney, Jessica Garvin, and others. They keep child sacrifice legal, and then play victim when criticized for it. At a certain point, the accusations and the people making them lose credibility.

questions.question: How did Silberman test whether his rhetoric was actually unchristlike?
questions.audience: pro-life Christians uncertain whether sharp rebuke is unchristlike
questions.answer: He took the criticism seriously enough to consult two pastors about whether his writing about Treat was out of line. Both pastors told him there was nothing wrong with what he said.
questions.quote: I thought hard about this. I want to be Christ-like, and getting called unchristlike from the floor of the Oklahoma Senate is a good time to examine whether I am doing so. I asked two pastors if they thought I was out of line regarding how I wrote about Treat. Neither thought there was anything wrong with what I said.

questions.question: What biblical basis does Silberman offer for sharply rebuking a politician?
questions.audience: pro-life Christians uncertain whether sharp rebuke is unchristlike
questions.answer: He cites Titus 1:10-16, which calls for sharp rebuke of those who teach for shameful gain what they ought not teach. He applies this to Treat for telling Oklahomans they must remain subservient to a federal regime that protects child sacrifice while branding his critics unchristlike.
questions.quote: One of them pointed me to Titus 1:10-16 which talks about sharply rebuking those who are “teaching for shameful gain what they ought not teach.” Treat is telling Oklahomans that they must remain subservient to federal opinions that child sacrifice should be legal. He is telling them that the Constitution gives the federal government unconditional authority over the states, and that upholding the right to life is unconstitutional. He is telling them this while demonizing as “unchristlike” those who are exhorting the State government to submit to Christ.

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe Treat slanders abolitionists?
questions.audience: Oklahoma voters evaluating Greg Treat's leadership
questions.answer: Silberman attributes Treat's hostility to political ambition: people who knew Treat say he was different before reaching the Capitol, and he suggests abolition bills threaten Treat's ladder-climbing, motivating him to slander FTS for personal gain.
questions.quote: People who have known Treat since he first became a Senator have said that he wasn’t always like this. Like many, he changed when he got to the Capitol because the allure of climbing the ladder is difficult to resist, though you often must ditch your principles to do it. Given his decision not to support the immediate abolition of abortion, abolition bills and Free the States are possibly impediments to Treat climbing the ladder. So he slanders us for personal gain. Treat is well-deserving of a sharp rebuke.

questions.question: Did Silberman's article contain obscenities Treat couldn't repeat on the Senate floor?
questions.audience: journalists covering church-state rhetoric in the abortion debate
questions.answer: No. Silberman says the two sentences Treat read were the harshest in the article, that there were no obscenities, and that Treat should publicly or privately correct the misleading impression he gave to colleagues.
questions.quote: In context, Treat seems to be referring to my article, insinuating that it contained obscene language that he couldn’t utter on the Senate floor. This is false. There were no obscenities, and the two sentences he mentioned were the harshest sentences in the article by far. Treat may have been talking about other emails he was receiving at the time or some other article, but most listeners will get the impression Treat is talking about my article, as that’s what he was talking about immediately prior. He should correct the record, either publicly or privately with his colleagues that there were no obscenities in my article.

questions.question: Does Silberman regret being harsh toward Treat?
questions.audience: abolitionist activists weighing how harshly to confront politicians
questions.answer: He defends the harshness as righteous given Treat's role in keeping abortion legal, but regrets giving Treat two extractable sentences that allowed him to play victim and ignore the substantive 2,100-word refutation.
questions.quote: I was harsh, but I was harsh against the man primarily responsible for keeping abortion legal in Oklahoma, who consistently spreads falsehoods about us and about the bill to abolish abortion. That is no sin. If I were to speak as God did about people who denied justice to the fatherless, I would have spoken much more harshly of Greg Treat. What I regret is that Treat was able to pull out two harsh sentences, when I worked hard to write a 2,100-word refutation of his position. In the future, I won’t give him the two sentences that he wants to pick out of my writing so he can play victim on the Senate floor.

questions.question: Why does Silberman conclude that severe language remains necessary in the abortion debate?
questions.audience: state legislators considering nullification bills like HB1236
questions.answer: He argues that as long as God's deacons in state government continue to approve of abortion, intense rhetorical heat is required to melt the 'mountains of ice' of indifference and complicity.
questions.quote: In our criticisms of politicians, [a little bit (or even a lot) of heat is necessary because there are “mountains of ice about us to melt.”] Until abortion is no longer approved of by God’s deacons in the Oklahoma State government, there is great need to be harsh.
